revision.text:
[[fr:Ichishi no Nagaochi]][[ja:市磯長尾市]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Iso no Sukune|qid=Q134863200|1=Iso no Sukune}}; {{ill|Yashiro no Sukune|qid=Q134864096|1=Yashiro no Sukune}}|father={{ill|Mimo no sukune|qid=Q134847980|1=Mimo no sukune}}}} {{infobox royalty| | father = '''{{ill|Mimo no sukune|ja|御物宿祢|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimo no sukune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' | issue = '''{{ill|Iso no Sukune|ja|五十野宿祢|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iso no Sukune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491578|1=Iso no Sukune}}''', {{ill|Yashiro no Sukune|ja|矢代宿祢|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro no Sukune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = Shinto | image = Ohyamato-jinja01s3200.jpg | caption = He was the first priest of [[Ōyamato Shrine]] |house=[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|predecessor={{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}|successor='''{{ill|Iso no Sukune|ja|五十野宿祢|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iso no Sukune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491578|1=Iso no Sukune}}'''}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} was a Japanese mythological figure. He was the first priest of [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]]..{{Cite web|title=［ID:8359］ 大和神社 Ōyamato Jinja ： 資料情報 {{!}} 研究資料・収蔵品データベース {{!}} 國學院大學デジタル・ミュージアム|url=https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=8359|access-date=2024-03-21|website=國學院大學デジタル・ミュージアム - 研究資料・収蔵品データベース|language=ja}}https://rest.neptune-prod.its.unimelb.edu.au/server/api/core/bitstreams/4bdbccdb-5218-5151-b8d1-7199ac126bf4/content He is the traditional ancestor of the {{ill|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qid=Q11387078|lt=Yamato clan|1=Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} as per the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]. He is a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|8=fr|lt2=珍彦命|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} == Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.]] Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|qid=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}} In the reign of Emperor Sujin there was a major epidemic and political crisis.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} To resolve the crisis he decided to enshrine the two gods separately with his two daughters as their priestesses. Amaterasu was entrusted to {{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}} and eventually moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. {{efn|Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} (Nara), where a [[Himorogi|Himorogi]] altar was built out of solid stone.
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi|1=Ōmi Province}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino|1=Mino Province}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|qid=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}}, where she received a revelation from Amaterasu: {{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an [[:en:Abstinence Palace|Abstinence Palace]] was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176}}}} This account serves as the origin myth of the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]], Amaterasu's chief place of worship. Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first [[Saiō|Ise Saiō]],{{cite web | url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 }}entrusting with her the mirror and sword, she brought them to the village of Kasanuhi. {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}}} while {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} was entrusted to {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but her health began to fail shortly afterward, and the plague didn't stop. These events still did not alleviate the ongoing plague sweeping the empire, so Sujin decreed a {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|qid=Q1043197|lt=Divination|1=Divination}} to be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign to find a solution. Sujin's aunt {{Nihongo|{{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qid=Q11387098|lt=Yamatototohimomoso-hime|1=Yamato-totohi-momoso-hime}}|倭迹迹日百襲媛命}} (daughter of 7th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|qid=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}) acted as a ''[[miko]]'', and was possessed by a god who identified himself as [[Ōmononushi]],{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042}} possibly the same entity as {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}} and be the first priest of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/files/6807/pdf_files/68996.pdf{{Cite web|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697/Book V - Wikisource, the free online library|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|access-date=2024-03-21|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the {{ill|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qid=Q11387078|lt=Yamato clan|1=Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. This god claimed responsibility for the plague, announcing that it would not stop until he was venerated. Although the Emperor propitiated to the god, the effects were not immediate. Sujin was later given guidance in the form of a dream to seek out a man named {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ōtataneko|ja|大田田根子|1=Ōtataneko|4=fr|5=Ōtataneko命|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FR}}|太田田根子}} and appoint him as head priest. He eventually found him in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}. When he was found and installed, the pestilence eventually subsided, allowing five cereal crops to ripen. Out of an abundance of caution, the Emperor also appointed {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ikagashikoo|ja|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashiko-o|qq=}}|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o}} as {{Nihongo|''kami-no-mono-akatsu-hito''|神班物者}}, or one who sorts the offerings to the gods.{{cite book |author=Chamberlain, Basil |url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj072.htm |title=''[SECT. LXV.—EMPEROR SŪ-JIN (PART III: STORY OF OHO-TATA-NE-KO'S BIRTH)]'' (''The Kojiki'') |publisher=Read before the Asiatic Society of Japan on April 12, May 10, and June 21, 1882, reprinted in 1919 |page=219 |quote=His Augustness Oho-tata-ne-ko ... was the ancestor of the Dukes of Miwa and of the Dukes of Kamo. |author-link=Basil Chamberlain}} To this day the {{ill|Miwa clan|lt=Miwa sept|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} of the {{ill|Kamo clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} claim to be descendants of {{ill|Ōtataneko|ja|大田田根子|1=Ōtataneko|4=fr|5=Ōtataneko命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FR}}, while {{ill|Ikagashikoo|ja|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashiko-o|qq=}} was a claimed ancestor of the now extinct {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|qq=}}. This has been suggested as representing a population {{ill|Migration|en|Migration|qid=Q1932583|lt=migration|1=Migration}} from {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}.{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8 |pages=152–168|doi=10.1515/9780824862848|qq=draft}} ==overview== {{Infobox Ancient Noble | Name = Ichishi no Nagaochi Sukuné (市磯長尾市宿禰) | image = | image_size = | Caption = | Era = [[Kofun period]] | Birth_date = Unknown | Death_date = Unknown | Alias = Ichishi no Nagaochi | Burial_place = | Official_Rank = {{ill|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qid=Q11387078|lt=Wakoku-no-Miyatsuko|1=Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} | Lord = {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} | Clan = Ancestor of the {{ill|Yamato clan (倭氏)|ja|倭氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato clan (倭氏)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Parents = | Siblings = | Children = | Notable_facts = }} '''Ichishi no Nagaochi Sukuné''' (市磯長尾市宿禰) was an ancient noble of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. He served as one of the '''{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|en|Kuni no miyatsuko|qid=Q2483673|lt=Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}}''' under the Yamato court. He is also called '''Ichishi no Nagaochi''' in some sources. He is revered as an ancestor of the {{ill|Yamato clan (倭氏)|ja|倭氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato clan (倭氏)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and is associated with the legend of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Ōkunitama no Kami|1=Yamato Okunitama}} (the deity enshrined at {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|en|Ōyamato Shrine|qid=Q245731|lt=Ōyamato Shrine|1=Ōyamato Shrine}} in modern-day {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}). Unlike other notable figures of the period, he is absent from the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. == Records == [[File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|en|Ōyamato Shrine|qid=Q245731|lt=Ōyamato Shrine|1=Ōyamato Shrine}} ({{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}})]] According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, in {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}'s 7th year, on the 8th day of the 7th month, three individuals—{{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qid=Q11387098|lt=Yamato Totohibi Momoso-hime|1=Yamato-totohi-momoso-hime}}, {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Sukune|ja|大水口宿禰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Sukune|1=Oomizuguchi no Sukune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11436785}}, and {{ill|Ise Omi no Kimi|ja|伊勢麻績君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Omi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—dreamt that the worship of {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (later enshrined at {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Ōkunitama no Kami|1=Yamato Okunitama}} (later enshrined at {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|en|Ōyamato Shrine|qid=Q245731|lt=Ōyamato Shrine|1=Ōyamato Shrine}}) should be entrusted to {{ill|Ōtataneko|ja|大田田根子|1=Ōtataneko|4=fr|5=Ōtataneko命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FR}} and Ichishi no Nagaochi, respectively. They reported this to the emperor, and following this revelation, rituals were held under their stewardship. The rituals successfully quelled the epidemic and brought peace to the land.{{Sfn|Ichishi no Nagaochi (Ancient Lineage)|2010}} In another account from the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} (year 3, third month), Ichishi no Nagaochi and {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomo|ja|大三輪大友主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Ōtomo|1=Otomoneshi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55536810}} were ordered to investigate the claims of {{ill|Amenohiboko|ja|天日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohiboko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノヒボコ}} (a prince from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}) in the {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}.{{Sfn|Ichishi no Nagaochi (Ancient Lineage)|2010}} Moreover, in Emperor Suinin's 7th year, Nagaochi was tasked with selecting {{ill|Nomi no Sukune|en|Nomi no Sukune|qid=Q965742|lt=Nominosukune|1=Nomi no Sukune}} from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge {{ill|Taima no Kehaya|ja|当麻蹶速|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taima no Kehaya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻蹴速|1=Taima no Kehaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487860}} in sumo wrestling, cementing his role as an influential figure.{{Sfn|Ichishi no Nagaochi (Ancient Lineage)|2010}} == Examination == The name "Ichishi" is believed to refer to a locality in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}, specifically in {{ill|Totsu District|ja|十市郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Totsu District|1=Toichi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11405036}} (modern-day {{ill|Ikuno|ja|池之内|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuno|1=ikenouchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q42581473}}, {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}).{{Sfn|Ichishi no Nagaochi (National History)}} {{Sfn|Ichishi no Nagaochi (Ancient Lineage)|2010}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=National History Encyclopedia|editor=Ueda Masaaki|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|chapter=Ichishi no Nagaochi|isbn=|ref={{Harv|Ichishi no Nagaochi (National History)}}}} * {{Cite book|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Japanese Lineages, Popular Edition|year=2010|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|chapter=Ichishi no Nagaochi|isbn=9784642014588|ref={{Harv|Ichishi no Nagaochi (Ancient Lineage)|2010}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Ōkunitama no Kami|1=Yamato Okunitama}} * {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|en|Ōyamato Shrine|qid=Q245731|lt=Ōyamato Shrine|1=Ōyamato Shrine}} - {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} * {{ill|Yamato Ōkunitama Shrine|ja|倭大国魂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōkunitama Shrine|1=Yamato Okunitama Shrine, Mima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q43594549}} - {{ill|Mima, Tokushima|en|Mima, Tokushima|qid=Q857828|lt=Mima|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|qid=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}} * {{ill|List of figures before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of figures before the Asuka period|1=list of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665973}} ==Genealogy== {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|qq=}} ==Notes== {{notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence
